---
title: "Advanced Manual: Recreating a Full Quarto Personal Site"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
---

## Goal

This manual explains how to recreate the broader feature set of the original Andrew Heiss site using your own content, voice, branding, and infrastructure.

The original site was not just a collection of pages. It used a consistent architecture:

- one global site config
- multiple content types
- custom listing templates
- custom SCSS styling
- reusable metadata
- separate patterns for posts, research, talks, and teaching
- static-file hosting for PDFs and assets
- feed generation
- manual deployment to a server

You can reproduce all of that without copying the content.

## Site Architecture as a System

The original site in [`/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original) was organized around content models.

### Content model 1: Standard pages

Examples:

- homepage
- CV
- 404 page
- now page
- uses page
- AI page

These are hand-written pages where layout and prose matter more than repeated structure.

### Content model 2: Blog-like dated posts

Example in the original site:

- [`/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/blog/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/blog/index.qmd)
- dated subfolders with `index.qmd` or `index.md`

Use this pattern for:

- essays
- tutorials
- technical notes
- public writing
- ongoing reflective series

### Content model 3: Research entries with rich metadata

Example in the original site:

- [`/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/research/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/research/index.qmd)
- item pages like [`/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/research/articles/heiss-2012/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/research/articles/heiss-2012/index.qmd)

Use this pattern when each item needs:

- a landing page
- citation text
- PDF links
- code links
- abstract
- appendix, slides, or replication materials

### Content model 4: Structured lists from YAML

Examples in the original site:

- teaching year files
- talks year files

Use this pattern when entries are mostly metadata and do not need a full page each.

## The Global Config Layer

[`_quarto.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/_quarto.yml) is the spine of the site.

Important sections to understand:

### `project`

Controls the site type, preview behavior, render scope, and resources.

Example:

```yaml
project:
  type: website
  render:
    - "*.qmd"
    - "*.md"
  resources:
    - "files/**"
    - ".htaccess"
```

Use `resources` for files Quarto should copy as-is.

### `website`

Controls:

- title
- description
- navbar
- footer
- site URL
- repo URL
- analytics
- social metadata

The original site had richer settings than your starter version. You can add them back later, for example:

```yaml
website:
  site-url: "https://yourdomain.com"
  repo-url: "https://github.com/yourname/your-site"
  google-analytics: "G-XXXXXXXXXX"
  twitter-card:
    image: "/files/social-card.png"
    card-style: summary_large_image
  open-graph:
    image: "/files/social-card.png"
    locale: en_US
```

### `format.html`

Controls the default HTML appearance for every page.

This is where your theme file is attached:

```yaml
format:
  html:
    theme:
      - html/ath.scss
```

## Reusing the Original Theme Correctly

The original site’s design lives mostly in [`html/ath.scss`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html/ath.scss).

That file controls:

- color palette
- fonts
- navbar colors
- footer styling
- code colors
- blog category sidebar
- responsive layout rules
- styling for custom listing components

If you want to keep the structure but make it yours, edit these first:

- `$purple`, `$pink`, `$red`, `$orange`, `$light-orange`
- `$font-family-sans-serif`
- `$headings-font-family`
- `$navbar-bg`
- `$link-color`
- `$footer-bg`

A safe workflow:

1. duplicate the current SCSS file first
2. change only colors and fonts
3. preview the whole site
4. then change component-specific rules

## Listing Pages: The Most Important Advanced Feature

The original site relies heavily on Quarto listings. This is what lets one page automatically surface many child entries.

### Basic listing anatomy

```yaml
listing:
  - id: posts
    contents: "2026/**/index.qmd"
    sort: "date desc"
    template: ../html/blog/listing.ejs
```

Then later in the page body:

```markdown
:::{#posts}
:::
```

This tells Quarto:

- which files to include
- how to sort them
- which custom template should render them
- where to place the final output in the page

### Why custom templates matter

The original site did not rely on the default Quarto listing look. It used EJS templates in [`html/`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html) to control the markup.

That gives you custom cards, metadata blocks, icons, category chips, thumbnails, and link sections.

If you want new content types later, you can create additional templates like:

- `html/projects/listing.ejs`
- `html/press/listing.ejs`
- `html/courses/listing.ejs`

## Rebuilding the Original Sections in Your Own Image

### About / homepage

What the original did:

- banner color
- profile photo
- two-column intro layout
- links to major work areas

How to recreate:

- keep the grid layout in [`index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/index.qmd)
- add your own headshot in `files/`
- use `<picture>` only if you want different mobile and desktop crops
- include a short bio plus one dynamic element, such as news

### CV

What the original did:

- embedded PDF
- download button
- custom CSS for iframe container

How to recreate:

- export your CV to PDF
- store it in `cv/` or `files/`
- use button + iframe embed if you want a PDF-first CV
- or write the whole CV directly in Quarto if you want an HTML-native CV

### Intuition / blog

What the original did:

- used dated folders
- used categories
- optionally used image thumbnails
- supported DOI display
- included tutorial-like posts with external code repos and books

How to recreate:

- keep the dated-post structure
- use `description` for preview text
- use `categories` for filtering
- use `image` when you want thumbnail cards
- add external links to GitHub, docs, or companion sites in the post body

### Research

What the original did:

- separated entries by type: article, chapter, working paper, review, translation, dormant paper
- used a custom citation-like listing format
- gave each item a full details page
- linked to preprints, final versions, publisher pages, and PDFs

How to recreate:

Option A: keep one simple `projects/` bucket.

Option B: expand into subtypes later:

```text
research/
  articles/
  chapters/
  working-papers/
  reports/
  software/
  public-writing/
```

Then update [`research/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/research/index.qmd) with multiple listing blocks, just like the original site.

### Teaching

What the original did:

- grouped courses by academic year
- used logos or thumbnail graphics
- linked each semester instance to course sites
- used a custom card layout

How to recreate:

If your teaching history becomes large, split [`teaching/courses.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/teaching/courses.yml) into multiple yearly files:

```text
teaching/
  ay_2025_26.yml
  ay_2026_27.yml
  index.qmd
```

Then define multiple listings on the page.

### Talks

What the original did:

- grouped talks by year
- attached multiple link types
- linked slides, companion sites, video, podcast, or GitHub

How to recreate:

Once your talks list grows, split [`talks/talks.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/talks/talks.yml) by year and use multiple listing IDs.

### News feed

What your new site adds:

- a homepage update stream
- a dedicated archive page
- low-friction short-form updates

This is a good addition because it gives you a lightweight publishing lane between permanent pages and full posts.

## Advanced Front Matter Patterns

### Social cards and thumbnails

The original blog used fields like:

```yaml
image: "img/card.png"
twitter-card:
  image: "img/card.png"
open-graph:
  image: "img/card.png"
```

Use this whenever a page should look good when shared.

### Page-specific static resources

The original blog sometimes used:

```yaml
resources:
  - "_book/**"
  - "img/*"
```

Use `resources` when a page depends on files that Quarto might not automatically copy, such as:

- image folders
- generated mini-sites
- JavaScript bundles
- downloadable attachments

### Executable settings

The original research section used shared defaults in [`research/_metadata.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/research/_metadata.yml).

This is powerful because folders can inherit shared settings like:

- date format
- title banner color
- execution defaults
- template partials

Use folder-level `_metadata.yml` when many child pages should behave similarly.

## Images, Media, and Assets at Scale

A robust pattern is to decide asset scope deliberately.

### Keep assets beside the page when:

- only one page uses them
- they belong to one article, post, or project
- they include thumbnails, figures, or downloadable appendices

### Keep assets in `files/` when:

- many pages reuse them
- they are site-level assets
- they include icons, profile images, logos, or shared PDFs

### Recommended conventions

- `img/` inside a page folder for post-specific images
- `files/profile/` for headshots or profile cards
- `files/downloads/` for generic PDFs and handouts
- `files/social/` for social cards

## Integrating External Code and Repositories

A major strength of the original site is that it treats the website as a public index to a wider body of work.

Use these patterns.

### Pattern 1: Website page + GitHub repo

For a talk, tutorial, or package:

- website page summarizes what it is
- GitHub repo stores the code
- page links to repo and demo

### Pattern 2: Website page + companion site

For courses, workshops, and books:

- main site contains the catalog entry
- companion site contains the full teaching or project materials
- GitHub repo hosts source code separately

### Pattern 3: Website page + downloadable local files

For research:

- project page contains abstract and context
- local PDFs store preprints or appendices
- external links point to DOI, publisher, or replication repo

## Integrating Interactive Content

The original site included advanced Quarto features like:

- OJS chunks
- tutorial sub-sites
- project-specific static outputs

You can use three layers of interactivity.

### Layer 1: Static plots generated by R or Python

Good default choice.

### Layer 2: OJS / Observable Plot inside Quarto

Good for small dynamic charts and controls without a server.

### Layer 3: Separate apps or dashboards

Good when the project is too large for a standard page. Then link to:

- a Quarto dashboard
- a Shiny app
- a separate static app site

## Feeds and Syndication

The original site had Atom and RSS feed pages. Your starter site keeps that pattern.

Why keep them:

- readers can subscribe
- some automation tools can ingest them
- they make your writing portable and machine-readable

If you later want separate feeds for `news` and `intuition`, create additional feed files with different listing scopes.

## Custom Templates and Partial Overrides

The original research section used a title-block partial and custom listing templates.

This is the next level of Quarto customization.

Use custom templates when:

- you dislike the default listing appearance
- one content type needs special metadata formatting
- you want visually distinct sections without hand-formatting every entry

Do not overuse them. The best use case is repeated structure.

## Custom 404 Page

The original site had a styled custom 404 page in [`/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/404.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-original/404.qmd).

You already have a minimal version in [`404.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/404.qmd). You can make it more useful by adding:

- a link back home
- links to research, intuition, and CV
- a site search suggestion

## Domain, Hosting, and Deployment Patterns

### Option 1: GitHub Pages

Recommended if you want the cleanest public workflow.

Suggested setup:

- repo: `kai-cooper-site`
- custom domain: `www.yourdomain.com`
- GitHub Action runs `quarto render`
- action deploys `_site/`

### Option 2: Netlify or Cloudflare Pages

Recommended if you want easy previews for pull requests and dead-simple domain handling.

### Option 3: VPS or institutional server

Recommended if you want full control.

The original site used `rsync` to upload `_site/` to a remote server. That is reliable for static sites if you have shell access.

## A Recommended Evolution Path for Your Site

### Phase 1: Minimal but complete

- About
- CV
- Intuition
- Research
- Teaching
- Talks
- News

### Phase 2: Personalize brand and metadata

- colors
- fonts
- profile image
- domain
- social metadata
- analytics

### Phase 3: Expand content models

- yearly teaching files
- yearly talks files
- multiple research buckets
- downloads section
- project pages

### Phase 4: Advanced publication workflow

- GitHub Actions deployment
- auto-generated social images
- richer bibliography integration
- separate companion sites for major projects

## Feature Recreation Map

If you want to recreate a specific original-site feature, use this mapping.

- Two-column homepage intro: adapt [`index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/index.qmd)
- Navbar and footer: edit [`_quarto.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/_quarto.yml)
- Color palette and fonts: edit [`html/ath.scss`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html/ath.scss)
- Post archive with categories: adapt [`intuition/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/intuition/index.qmd) and [`html/blog/listing.ejs`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html/blog/listing.ejs)
- Research citations with buttons: adapt [`research/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/research/index.qmd) and [`html/research/listing.ejs`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html/research/listing.ejs)
- Teaching cards: adapt [`teaching/courses.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/teaching/courses.yml) and [`html/teaching/listing.ejs`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html/teaching/listing.ejs)
- Talks archive with slides and repo links: adapt [`talks/talks.yml`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/talks/talks.yml) and [`html/talks/listing.ejs`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/html/talks/listing.ejs)
- News feed on homepage: adapt [`news/index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/news/index.qmd) and [`index.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/index.qmd)
- Feed generation: edit [`atom.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/atom.qmd) and [`rss.qmd`](/Users/kaicooper/Documents/ath-quarto-kc/rss.qmd)

## Standards to Follow So the Site Stays Manageable

- One content type, one consistent structure.
- Keep repeated metadata in YAML, not hand-written HTML.
- Keep shared styling in SCSS, not scattered inline styles.
- Let listing pages generate archives automatically.
- Use local page folders for self-contained projects.
- Put only reusable assets in `files/`.
- Treat the website as an index to your broader scholarly and creative work.

## What to Build Next

The highest-value next steps are:

1. add your real homepage bio and headshot
2. decide whether your CV is PDF-first or HTML-first
3. define your research buckets
4. decide whether teaching and talks should stay single-file or become year-based
5. connect the site to a GitHub repository and deployment target
6. standardize how you will link code, data, slides, and downloads across the site
